Title: Dual Catalytic Activity of a Cytochrome P450 Controls Bifurcation at a Metabolic Branch Point of Alkaloid Biosynthesis in Rauwolfia serpentina

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to formation of perakine-derived Rauwolfia alkaloids
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Captures perakine and drives the pathway toward perakine-derived products in leaves
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression-metabolite correlation described in paper; no new direct functional characterization in this study
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Perakine reductase is highly expressed in leaf. 2. Its expression is proposed to capture perakine generated by VH. 3. This helps direct Rauwolfia leaf metabolism toward perakine-derived alkaloids.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript abundance/metabolite distribution data; accession NA
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because it is discussed as a downstream flux-partitioning enzyme, but it was not the main discovery of this paper.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ajmaline is an anti-arrhythmic agent used in diagnosis of Brugada syndrome
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides driving force for formation of dihydro-vomilenine and ajmaline in root
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression-metabolite correlation and prior pathway knowledge referenced in paper; no new direct functional characterization in this study
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Vomilenine reductase is highly expressed in young root. 2. Its expression is proposed to channel vomilenine toward dihydro-vomilenine and ajmaline. 3. Together with VH, it contributes to root-biased ajmaline biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcript abundance/metabolite distribution data; accession NA
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Two routes from vomilenine to ajmaline are proposed in the figure; this study did not newly characterize vomilenine reductase.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Rauwolfia; heterologous yeast assay system for functional testing
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoterpene indole alkaloid (MIA) / ajmaline-perakine branch biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branch point from vinorine/vomilenine toward ajmaline versus perakine-derived alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates vinorine at C-21 to form vomilenine, and also catalyzes redox-neutral isomerization of vomilenine to perakine
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Branch point; mid-to-downstream step controlling pathway bifurcation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of ajmaline and directly diverts flux toward perakine branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ajmaline is an anti-arrhythmic agent used in diagnosis of Brugada syndrome; perakine is a structurally distinct Rauwolfia alkaloid scaffold leading to perakine-derived alkaloids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Ajmaline accumulates highly in young roots; perakine and perakine-derived alkaloids accumulate in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in root tissues; also detected in leaf
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in roots, also found in leaf
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls bifurcation of alkaloid metabolism by producing vomilenine for ajmaline biosynthesis and perakine for the alternative branch
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Assay pH strongly affects product outcome; hydroxylation optimum around pH 6.5 and isomerization optimum around pH 4.5; NADPH required for hydroxylation but not for isomerization
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP candidate 5437 from the Rauwolfia transcriptome was identified as vinorine hydroxylase (VH/CYP82S18). 2. Recombinant VH hydroxylates vinorine to form vomilenine, filling a missing step in ajmaline biosynthesis. 3. VH also catalyzes non-oxidative isomerization of vomilenine to perakine, revealing dual catalytic activity at a metabolic branch point. 4. Tissue expression and downstream enzyme expression patterns help explain root-biased ajmaline and leaf-associated perakine branch products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative transcriptomics/metabolomics-guided candidate selection; heterologous expression in yeast with CPR; microsomal in vitro assays; substrate feeding; LC-MS; large-scale product isolation; NMR structural elucidation; authentic standard co-elution; pH and cofactor dependence tests
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-control step; decisive enzyme controlling bifurcation between ajmaline and perakine branches
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Cytochrome P450 reductase (CPR) in heterologous assay
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome and metabolomic datasets from Rauwolfia transcriptome resource (Medicinal Plant Genomics Resource, MPGR); accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dual activity may partition flux away from ajmaline toward perakine depending on tissue context and downstream enzyme expression
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Vomilenine exists as rapidly interconverting C-21 epimers; VH is highly substrate-specific; closest Catharanthus roseus homolog did not accept vinorine; perakine formation is directional and irreversible under assay conditions; unusual for a plant CYP to catalyze a non-oxidative isomerization
